passages.explanation: The decision is made for Paul and some other prisoners to sail for Italy. They're handed over to a centurion named Julius, of the Augustan Regiment. Luke is with them — the 'we' narration continues. They board a ship from Adramyttium and sail along the coast of the province of Asia. Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica, is also with them. At Sidon, Julius kindly allows Paul to visit friends and receive care. They sail under the lee of Cyprus because of headwinds, cross the open sea along Cilicia and Pamphylia, and reach Myra in Lycia. There Julius finds an Alexandrian ship sailing for Italy and transfers them onto it. They make slow progress against the wind, eventually reaching Fair Havens on the south coast of Crete. Luke's navigation details are remarkably precise — this is one of the most detailed ancient sea voyage accounts we have.

Much time has been lost, and sailing has become dangerous because the Fast (the Day of Atonement, around October) has already passed — the Mediterranean shipping season typically ended by mid-November. Paul warns them: I can see this voyage is going to be disastrous, with great loss to ship, cargo, and our lives. But the centurion follows the advice of the pilot and ship owner instead of Paul. Fair Havens isn't a good harbour to winter in, so the majority decide to press on for Phoenix, a better harbour further along Crete's coast. A prisoner's advice is weighed against professional mariners' judgment. The professionals are wrong.

A gentle south wind begins to blow, and they think they've got what they need. They weigh anchor and sail close to the shore of Crete. Before long, a hurricane-force wind called the Northeaster sweeps down from the island. The ship is caught and can't head into the wind, so they give way and are driven along. They manage to secure the ship's boat, pass ropes under the ship to hold it together, and lower the sea anchor. The next day they throw cargo overboard. On the third day they throw the ship's tackle over with their own hands. When neither sun nor stars appear for many days and the storm continues raging, they finally give up all hope of being saved. Luke's account is vivid and terrifying — total darkness, no navigation, the crew literally discarding everything to survive.

After the men have gone a long time without food, Paul stands up among them. He tells them: you should have taken my advice and not sailed from Crete — you would have avoided this damage and loss. But take courage — not one of you will be lost, only the ship. Last night an angel of the God to whom I belong and whom I serve stood beside me and said: don't be afraid, Paul. You must stand trial before Caesar, and God has graciously given you the lives of all who sail with you. So keep up your courage — I have faith in God that it will happen exactly as he told me. Nevertheless, we must run aground on some island. Paul's authority has shifted: the prisoner is now the voice of hope on a doomed ship. His faith isn't blind optimism — it's rooted in a specific divine promise.

On the fourteenth night, they're being driven across the Adriatic Sea. Around midnight the sailors sense they're approaching land. They take soundings — twenty fathoms, then fifteen. Fearing the ship will hit rocks, they drop four anchors from the stern and pray for daylight. The sailors try to escape by lowering the lifeboat, pretending to set anchors from the bow. Paul tells the centurion and soldiers: unless these men stay with the ship, you cannot be saved. The soldiers cut the ropes and let the lifeboat drift away. Paul's word now carries operational authority. The centurion who ignored him before now acts on his every instruction.

Just before dawn, Paul urges everyone to eat: for fourteen days you've been in constant suspense and have gone without food. I urge you to eat — you need it to survive. Not one of you will lose a single hair from your head. He takes bread, gives thanks to God in front of them all, breaks it, and begins to eat. They're all encouraged and eat as well. There are 276 people on board. After eating, they lighten the ship by throwing the grain into the sea. The scene echoes communion — Paul gives thanks, breaks bread, and shares it. On a pagan ship in a crisis, Paul's faith is visible and contagious.

At daylight they don't recognise the land, but they see a bay with a sandy beach and decide to run the ship aground there. They cut the anchors loose, untie the rudders, hoist the foresail, and head for the beach. The ship strikes a sandbar — the bow sticks fast and the stern is broken apart by the pounding surf. The soldiers plan to kill the prisoners to prevent escape — standard procedure, since they'd pay with their own lives. But the centurion wants to save Paul and stops them. He orders those who can swim to jump overboard first, and the rest to grab planks or pieces of the ship. Everyone reaches land safely — all 276. Not a single life lost, exactly as Paul said. The ship is destroyed; the people are saved. They've landed on Malta.
